# Release Scaffolding Guide

> **Production-like releases are a process, not a button**: build issue intake, versioning, changelog discipline, and GitHub release automation together.

---

## Overview

Release scaffolding is the set of repository artifacts that make future work
trackable, releasable, and explainable. A repo with strong release scaffolding:

- captures work through structured issue intake
- routes new work into the right project or backlog
- uses consistent labels for release-note categorization
- records human-authored history in `CHANGELOG.md`
- publishes GitHub Releases with repeatable automation
- gives maintainers a documented release checklist

This guide is intentionally generic so it can be applied to any GitHub
repository, regardless of language or framework.

---

## When to Add Release Scaffolding

Add release scaffolding when any of the following becomes true:

- multiple maintainers are contributing to the repo
- end users need to understand what changed between versions
- work is being tracked in GitHub Issues or Projects
- the repo is distributed as a package, plugin, SDK, service, or reusable tool
- fixes, workarounds, or upgrade notes need a durable home

Do not wait until the first “real release.” Release scaffolding is most useful
when it exists before the release pressure arrives.

---

## Recommended Artifact Set

For a production-like release pattern, create all of the following together:

1. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`
   Structured issue forms or templates for bugs, features, and documentation.
2. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml`
   Issue template chooser configuration and contact links.
3. `CHANGELOG.md`
   Human-maintained release history and operator-facing notes.
4. `.github/release.yml`
   GitHub autogenerated release note categories and exclusions.
5. `.github/workflows/release.yml`
   Tag-driven release automation.
6. `RELEASING.md`
   Maintainer playbook for versioning, tagging, validation, and post-release review.

Treat these as one slice of work. A changelog without a release flow, or issue
forms without project routing, leaves the process half-finished.

---

## Part 1: Issue Intake Scaffolding

### Goals

Issue intake should make new work immediately actionable:

- classify the request
- apply default labels
- route the issue to the correct project or backlog
- collect enough context to avoid back-and-forth triage

### Preferred Structure

Create issue forms under:

```text
.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/
```

Recommended starter set:

- `bug-report.yml`
- `feature-request.yml`
- `documentation-request.yml`
- `config.yml`

### Supported GitHub Metadata

GitHub issue forms support top-level metadata such as:

- `labels`
- `assignees`
- `title`
- `type`
- `projects`

Use `projects` when you want issues created from the form to be added to a
GitHub Project automatically.

**Format**:

```yaml
projects:
  - OWNER/PROJECT_NUMBER
```

Example:

```yaml
name: Bug report
description: Report a reproducible bug.
title: "[Bug]: "
labels:
  - bug
projects:
  - your-org/8
body:
  - type: textarea
    id: summary
    attributes:
      label: Current behavior
    validations:
      required: true
```

### Important Limitation

GitHub documents that the person opening the issue must have write access to the
specified project for form-based `projects` routing to succeed.

If outside contributors are expected to open issues, also configure the
project-side **auto-add workflow** so issues can still be routed even when the
form-level `projects` key is insufficient.

### Issue Template Chooser

Use `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` to:

- disable blank issues when structured intake is required
- point users to support, docs, security reporting, or discussions

Example:

```yaml
blank_issues_enabled: false
contact_links:
  - name: Support and release notes
    url: https://example.com/support
    about: Review docs and release notes before opening a support issue.
```

### Label Design

Keep issue labels intentional. At minimum, define labels that support:

- bug tracking
- feature/enhancement tracking
- documentation work
- release-note grouping

Avoid using only workflow or urgency labels for release categorization. GitHub
release note automation works best when labels represent user-visible change
types.

---

## Part 2: Versioning and Changelog Discipline

### Versioning

Pick and document a versioning strategy. In most repos that publish something
externally, **Semantic Versioning** is the simplest default:

- `MAJOR`: breaking changes
- `MINOR`: backward-compatible features
- `PATCH`: backward-compatible fixes

The version should have one clear home in the repo, such as:

- `pyproject.toml`
- `package.json`
- `Cargo.toml`
- a dedicated version file

### Changelog

Use a durable `CHANGELOG.md` for notes that autogenerated release notes do not
capture well:

- upgrade cautions
- workarounds
- operational notes
- known limitations
- deprecations

Recommended structure:

```markdown
# Changelog

## [Unreleased]

### Added
- ...

### Changed
- ...

### Fixed
- ...

## [1.2.0] - 2026-04-14

### Added
- ...
```

Keep the changelog human-authored. Let GitHub generate contributor and merged PR
summaries, but do not outsource operator guidance to automation.

---

## Part 3: GitHub Release Notes Configuration

GitHub supports autogenerated release notes, and `.github/release.yml` lets you
control how pull requests are grouped.

Recommended categories:

- Features
- Fixes
- Documentation
- Other Changes

Example:

```yaml
changelog:
  exclude:
    labels:
      - ignore-for-release
  categories:
    - title: Features
      labels:
        - enhancement
    - title: Fixes
      labels:
        - bug
    - title: Documentation
      labels:
        - documentation
    - title: Other Changes
      labels:
        - "*"
```

This keeps release notes stable even as the team grows. It also creates a clear
reason to maintain clean label hygiene.

---

## Part 4: Release Workflow Automation

### Recommended Trigger

Use a version tag as the release event:

```yaml
on:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"
```

This keeps release publication explicit and auditable.

### Recommended GitHub CLI Pattern

Use `gh release create` with autogenerated notes:

```bash
gh release create "$TAG_NAME" \
  --title "$TAG_NAME" \
  --generate-notes \
  --latest
```

This allows:

- a predictable GitHub Release page
- autogenerated notes grouped by `.github/release.yml`
- a repeatable release event in Actions

### Example Workflow

```yaml
name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  github-release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Create GitHub release
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
          TAG_NAME: ${{ github.ref_name }}
        run: |
          gh release create "$TAG_NAME" \
            --title "$TAG_NAME" \
            --generate-notes \
            --latest
```

Consider adding an existence check if the workflow may be rerun manually.

---

## Part 5: Maintainer Release Playbook

Automated releases still need a human checklist. A lightweight `RELEASING.md`
should describe:

1. how to confirm the target branch is ready
2. which labels drive release categories
3. when `CHANGELOG.md` must be updated
4. where the canonical version is bumped
5. how to create and push the tag
6. how to review the GitHub Release page after publication

Example tagging flow:

```bash
git checkout main
git pull --ff-only
git tag vX.Y.Z
git push origin main
git push origin vX.Y.Z
```

The playbook should also say when a maintainer must manually edit the generated
release notes to add workarounds, migration notes, or production cautions.

---

## Recommended Rollout Order

When adding release scaffolding to an existing repo, use this order:

1. identify the target GitHub Project and release labels
2. add issue forms and chooser config
3. add `CHANGELOG.md`
4. add `.github/release.yml`
5. add `.github/workflows/release.yml`
6. add `RELEASING.md`
7. update PR templates so contributors remember release-note discipline
8. test the repo changes before publishing them

This sequence keeps issue intake and release output aligned from day one.

---

## Validation Checklist

Before merging release scaffolding:

- [ ] Issue forms render correctly in GitHub
- [ ] Default labels referenced by forms already exist
- [ ] The `projects` target uses `OWNER/PROJECT_NUMBER`
- [ ] `CHANGELOG.md` has an `Unreleased` section
- [ ] `.github/release.yml` categories map to real labels
- [ ] The release workflow is tag-driven, not branch-driven
- [ ] The release workflow uses `--generate-notes`
- [ ] `RELEASING.md` documents tagging and post-release review
- [ ] Repo tests or static checks cover the new scaffolding

---

## Common Failure Modes

### 1. Forms reference labels that do not exist

Result: issues are created without the intended label discipline.

Fix: create labels first or choose existing labels only.

### 2. `projects` routing does not work for contributors

Result: issues open successfully but do not land in the project.

Fix: confirm project permissions and enable project-side auto-add rules.

### 3. Release notes are noisy or badly grouped

Result: every release becomes a cleanup exercise.

Fix: tighten label hygiene and refine `.github/release.yml`.

### 4. The changelog becomes stale

Result: GitHub Releases and repo docs disagree.

Fix: add a PR checklist item requiring release notes / changelog updates for
user-visible changes.

---

## References

- GitHub Docs: issue forms syntax and template configuration
- GitHub Docs: automatically generated release notes
- GitHub Docs: managing releases in a repository

The exact GitHub features can evolve, so verify supported metadata and workflow
behavior against the current docs before standardizing the pattern across many
repositories.
